    Hostless Supernovae

    Hostless Supernovae

    Type:Research-level concept
    Definition:Supernovae with no detectable host galaxy at their location
    Related: Type Ia supernovae
    Large-offset supernovae
    Dynamical stellar displacement
    Collisional ring galaxies

    Hostless supernovae are transient events that appear to lack a detectable host galaxy at their explosion site. They are predominantly Type Ia supernovae, since the long delay times of SN Ia progenitors allow them to explode far from obvious star-forming regions[1][2][3].

    Proposed Explanations

    Several mechanisms have been invoked to explain apparently hostless SNe Ia:

    • Intracluster stars: Stars stripped from cluster galaxies into the diffuse intracluster medium can produce SNe Ia far from any individual galaxy[1][4].
    • Ultra-faint dwarf hosts: The host galaxy may exist but fall below the surface brightness detection limit of the survey[3][5].
    • Hypervelocity progenitors: Binary systems ejected at high velocity from their parent galaxy[5].
    • Dynamical displacement in mergers: Galaxy mergers can strip old stellar populations into tidal features or collisional rings that are too faint to detect at higher redshifts[6][7].

    The Surface Brightness Dimming Problem

    O'Connor et al. (2026) highlight that collisional ring structures like "Pika's Halo" would be undetectable at higher redshifts due to cosmological surface brightness dimming[6]. SN 2025adpq, clearly located within a collisional ring at z = 0.154, would appear "hostless" if observed at z > 0.3–0.5 where the low-surface-brightness ring falls below detection limits[6]. This represents a systematic bias in current surveys: some fraction of apparently hostless SNe Ia may reside in undetected tidal or ring structures[6][5].

    Why This Matters

    Hostless SN Ia classification affects cosmological analyses: host-galaxy corrections for SN Ia standardization cannot be applied when no host is identified, potentially introducing systematic biases in Hubble diagram measurements[6]. Rubin LSST, with its deeper imaging and larger survey area, is expected to detect low-surface-brightness features around many currently "hostless" events[8][6]. Targeted deep imaging of known hostless SN Ia sites could already reveal faint tidal structures or ring remnants[5].

    See Also

    • Large-offset supernovae
    • Type Ia supernovae
    • Dynamical stellar displacement
    • Collisional ring galaxies
    • O'Connor et al. (2026) — SN 2025adpq

    References
    1. Gal-Yam, A. et al. (2003). "Hostless Type Ia SNe and intracluster stars." AJ, 125, 1087.
    2. Graham, M. L. et al. (2015). "Apparently hostless Type Ia supernovae." MNRAS, 446, 2073.
    3. Qin, Y.-J. et al. (2024). "Hostless Type Ia SN environments."
    4. Sand, D. J. et al. (2011). "Large-offset Type Ia SNe in clusters."
    5. Strolger, L.-G. et al. (2025). "Searches for displaced populations in hostless SN Ia environments."
    6. O'Connor, B. et al. (2026). "SN 2025adpq." arXiv:2603.15899
    7. Ditrani, E. et al. (2024). "Redistribution of old progenitors by galaxy interactions."
    8. Ivezić, Ž. et al. (2019). "LSST: from science drivers to reference design." ApJ, 873, 111.
